title: Identification of Unobservable Behavior in Stochastic Discrete Event Systems
author: Santillán-Mosquera, Muñoz-Añasco

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: This section presents the proposed method's results in a case study. The case study is an industrial application with behavior that evolves from states and whose behavioral model is required as a DES. Its objective is to glue a soft pad over an armrest. The line has a conveyor, a glue injector with a sliding movement, a pad chute with an inclined belt, a pad dispenser and placer, a pad press station whit a vibrator, cylinders along the line that perform the function of barrier and traffic control, an automatic wrapper station, and an HMI screen where the parameterization, control, and supervision of the entire system is performed. The plant under study has a stochastic behavior and is instrumented with six sensors and 16 actuators which are listed to following:Sensors: Reflex Sensor 1, Reflex Sensor 2, Reflex Sensor 3, Reflex Sensor 4, Proximity Sensor 1 and Proximity Sensor 2.Actuators: Barrier Cylinder 1, Barrier Cylinder 2, Run Glue Injector, Glue spread Cylinder, Barrier Cylinder 3, Barrier Cylinder 4, Positioner Cylinder 1, Pad Placer cylinder, Barrier Cylinder 5, Press Cylinder, Positioner Cylinder 2, Run vibration and Run spinner wrapper.

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: It is desired to find the model that represents the observable and unobservable behavior as a DES. For this purpose, it is proposed to use the method presented in this article.

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: Applying Algorithm 1 to the observed sequences, reported in Table 2, the resulting st-IPN is shown in Fig. 5, representing the observable behavior.Algorithm 1Algorithm for identification of the observable st-IPN.Algorithm 1Inputseqqobs=ω0ω1…ωk;ωi=(usi,yji)(μi,σi).OutputPre;Post;φ(P);λ(TR)Initialconditions:np=1;ntr=0;φ(p1)=yj0;Pre=[];Post=[].fori=1:kk:numberofeventsreadωi=(usi,yji)(μi,σi)ifyji≠yji−1∀ithennp=np+1;(newplace)φ(pnp+1)=yji;ntr=ntr+1;λ(trntr)=usi(μi,σi);pre⇀≔zeros(np+1,ntr):pre⇀(np−1,ntr)=1;updatePrewithpre⇀;post⇀≔zeros(np+1,ntr);post⇀(np,ntr)=1;updatePostwithpost⇀;cp=np;ctr=ntr.elseifexistingplace;pre⇀=Pre(:,ctr);ntr′=ntr+1;λ(trntr′)=usi(μi,σi);forr=1:ntrifλ(trr)=λ(trntr′)andpre⇀(cp,r)=1andpost⇀(cp,r)=1thenbreak(existingplace)elseifλ(trr)≠λ(trntr′)∧pre⇀(cp,r)=1∧post⇀(cp,r)=1thenntr=ntr′(newtransition)updatePrewithpre⇀;post⇀=Post(:,ntr);post⇀(cp,ntr)=1;λ(trntr)=λ(trntr′)endifendforendifendforFig. 5st-IPN of observable behavior of the case study.Fig 5

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: The transition eliminated by transition self-loops due to settling time is tr5.

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: The st-IPN with observable and unobservable behavior is shown in Fig. 6.Fig. 6st-IPN of observable and unobservable behavior of the case study.Fig 6

description: The proposed method consists of two stages: the identification of the structure of an st-IPN (see Definition 7) from sequences of timed events observed as defined in Definition 6, of a system as described in Definition 2, and then the addition of the unobservable behavior to the structure of the identified st-IPN based on the projection of languages. Unlike reference [1], In the present proposal the system is not divided into subsystems; furthermore, the identification algorithm works offline.
